reasoning: The value of the ICE BofA US Corporate Index Option-Adjusted Spread just jumped to 0.82 on Friday, August 1, 2025. This uptick coincided with highly concerning economic data: a dismal July jobs report showing only 73,000 jobs added alongside massive negative revisions of 258,000 for prior months, plus new tariff announcements from the Trump administration. This risk-off 'shock' is highly stagflationary, a classic trigger for widening corporate bond spreads as credit risk rises and investors flee to safety. Given that 0.82 is still incredibly tight by historical standards (the floor over the last year has held firmly around 0.78), asymmetric risk strongly points to further widening. In the immediate and short-term (August 10, September 2), spreads are very likely to remain elevated above 0.82 as the market prices in this new economic stress. However, as the horizon expands to 3 and 6 months (November 2025 and January 2026), central bank interventions and cyclical market easing could cause spreads to mean-revert or compress back down. Adhering to the prompt’s directive, uncertainty increases over longer forecast horizons, pulling probabilities closer to 0.5.
